podcasts.name: Lenny's Podcast
podcasts.subtitle: Product | Career | Growth
podcasts.blurb: 面向产品和创业从业者，聚焦增长策略、职业发展与顶尖实践。

episodes.title: Head of Growth (Anthropic): “Claude is growing itself at this point” | Amol Avasare
episodes.description:
Amol Avasare is Head of Growth at Anthropic, which is going through the most unprecedented growth trajectory in history—scaling from $1 billion to over $19 billion in ARR in just 14 months. Previously, Amol worked on the growth teams at Mercury and MasterClass. Before that he was a founder, and he cold emailed his way into the Anthropic role when no job listing existed. Most remarkably, he overcame a traumatic brain injury from a Muay Thai match that meant he couldn't work for nearly a year.
In our in-depth discussion, Amol shares:
1. How Amol landed his role by cold emailing Anthropic’s CPO Mike Krieger
2. How Anthropic is automating growth experiments with Claude (their internal tool called “CASH”)
3. Why the ratio of PMs to engineers might need to flip (more PMs than engineers) as AI makes engineers exponentially more productive
4. Why activation is the single highest-leverage growth problem in AI
5. Why Anthropic indexes 70/30 toward big bets (the opposite of most growth teams)
6. How he uses Cowork to detect team misalignment in Slack
7. How the company’s focus on AI coding created a research flywheel that accelerated their models
episodes.descriptionCN:
Amol Avasare 是 Anthropic 的增长主管，该公司正在经历历史上最前所未有的增长轨迹——ARR 在短短 14 个月内从 10 亿美元扩大到超过 190 亿美元。此前，Amol 曾在 Mercury 和 MasterClass 的增长团队工作。在此之前，他是一名创始人，在没有职位空缺的情况下，他通过冷冰冰的电子邮件进入了人类角色。最引人注目的是，他克服了泰拳比赛造成的脑外伤，这意味着他近一年无法工作。
在我们的深入讨论中，Amol 分享了：
1. Amol 是如何通过向 Anthropic 的 CPO Mike Krieger 发送冷邮件而获得职位的
2. Anthropic 如何与 Claude 一起自动化生长实验（他们的内部工具称为“CASH”）
3. 为什么随着人工智能使工程师的工作效率成倍提高，PM 与工程师的比例可能需要调整（PM 的数量多于工程师）
4. 为什么激活是人工智能中杠杆率最高的单一增长问题
5. 为什么 Anthropic 对大赌注的指数为 70/30（与大多数增长团队相反）
6. 他如何使用 Cowork 来检测 Slack 中的团队失调
7. 该公司对人工智能编码的关注如何创建了一个研究飞轮来加速他们的模型

episodes.title: An AI state of the union: We’ve passed the inflection point, dark factories are coming, and automation timelines | Simon Willison
episodes.description:
Simon Willison is a prolific independent software developer, a blogger, and one of the most visible and trusted voices on the impact AI is having on builders. He co-created Django, the web framework that powers Instagram, Pinterest, and tens of thousands of other websites. He coined the term “prompt injection,” popularized the terms “AI slop” and “agentic engineering,” and has built over 100 open source projects, including Datasette, a data analysis tool used by investigative journalists worldwide. What makes Simon unique is that he’s made the leap from traditional software engineering to AI-native development more fully and visibly than almost anyone—and he’s been documenting everything he learns in real time on his blog, SimonWillison.net.
In our in-depth conversation, Simon shares:
1. Why November 2025 was the inflection point when AI coding agents crossed from “mostly works” to “actually works”
2. How Simon writes 95% of his code from his phone now and why he’s mentally exhausted by 11 a.m.
3. Why mid-career engineers (not juniors) are most at risk right now
4. The three agentic engineering patterns Simon uses daily (red/green TDD, templates, hoarding)
5. The next leap: the “dark factory” pattern where nobody writes or reviews code and AI does its own QA
6. Why prompt injection is an unsolved security problem and the “lethal trifecta” that will likely lead to an AI Challenger disaster
7. Why the pelican riding a bicycle became the unofficial benchmark for AI model quality
episodes.descriptionCN:
Simon Willison 是一位多产的独立软件开发人员、博主，也是人工智能对建筑商影响方面最引人注目、最值得信赖的声音之一。他共同创建了 Django，这是一个为 Instagram、Pinterest 和数以万计的其他网站提供支持的 Web 框架。他创造了“即时注入”一词，推广了“AI slop”和“代理工程”等术语，并构建了 100 多个开源项目，其中包括 Datasette，这是一种全球调查记者使用的数据分析工具。 Simon 的独特之处在于，他比几乎任何人都更全面、更明显地实现了从传统软件工程到 AI 原生开发的飞跃，而且他一直在他的博客 SimonWillison.net 上实���记录他学到的一切。
在我们的深入对话中，西蒙分享了：
1. 为什么 2025 年 11 月是 AI 编码代理从“大部分有效”跨越到“实际有效”的拐点
2. Simon 现在 95% 的代码是如何通过手机编写的，以及为什么他到了上午 11 点就精神疲惫。
3. 为什么处于职业生涯中期的工程师（而不是初级工程师）现在面临的风险最大
4. Simon 日常使用的三种代理工程模式（红/绿 TDD、模板、囤积）
5. 下一个飞跃：“黑暗工厂”模式，没有人编写或审查代码，人工智能自己进行质量检查
6. 为什么即时注入是一个未解决的安全问题以及可能导致 AI Challenger 灾难的“致命三连击”
7. 为什么骑自行车的鹈鹕成为AI模型质量的非官方标杆

episodes.title: From skeptic to true believer: How OpenClaw changed my life | Claire Vo
episodes.description:
Claire Vo is the host of our sister podcast, “How I AI,” a former product executive and engineer, and founder of an AI startup called ChatPRD. Claire now runs her business, podcast, and family life with the help of nine OpenClaw agents running on multiple Mac Minis and old laptops. In this episode, Claire shares her journey from OpenClaw skeptic (it deleted her family calendar the first time she tried it) to true believer, and gives a masterclass in using AI agents in real life.
We discuss:
1. The exact step-by-step process to install and set up OpenClaw (it’s easier than you think)
2. How to avoid the biggest OpenClaw mistakes (don’t install it on your main computer)
3. Actual use cases that have changed Claire’s life (e.g. family scheduling, inbound sales, podcast prep, and course management)
4. Why multiple specialized agents beat one general-purpose agent
5. The security risks everyone worries about—and how to handle them
6. Browser limitations, memory issues, and practical workarounds
episodes.descriptionCN:
Claire Vo 是我们的姐妹播客“How I AI”的主持人，她曾担任产品主管和工程师，也是一家名为 ChatPRD 的人工智能初创公司的创始人。 Claire 现在在多台 Mac Mini 和旧笔记本电脑上运行的 9 个 OpenClaw 代理的帮助下经营她的生意、播客和家庭生活。在本集中，克莱尔分享了她从 OpenClaw 怀疑论者（她第一次尝试时就删除了她的家庭日历）到真正的信徒的旅程，并提供了在现实生活中使用人工智能代理的大师班。
我们讨论：
1. 安装和设置 OpenClaw 的确切分步过程（比您���象的更容易）
2. 如何避免最大的 OpenClaw 错误（不要将其安装在您的主计算机上）
3. 改变克莱尔生活的实际用例（例如家庭安排、入站销售、播客准备和课程管理）
4. 为什么多个专业代理胜过一个通用代理
5. 每个人都担心的安全风险以及如何应对
6. 浏览器限制、内存问题和实际解决方法

episodes.title: The art of influence: The single most important skill that AI can’t replace | Jessica Fain (Webflow, ex-Slack)
episodes.description:
Jessica Fain is a product leader at Webflow and former Chief of Staff to the CPO at Slack, where she worked alongside April Underwood and many past podcast guests including Stewart Butterfield, Annie Pearl, Tamar Yehoshua, and Noah Weiss. She’s spent her career learning how executives actually make decisions—and why most people completely misunderstand the process.
We discuss:
1. Why great ideas often don’t get buy-in
2. Why executive calendars are “like strobe lights” and why the first 30 seconds of a meeting matter so much
3. Why executives are usually optimizing for a global maximum while you are often optimizing locally
4. The best question Jessica uses when a leader says something that seems wrong: “That’s so interesting. What led you to believe that?”
5. Why you should go in to learn, not to convince
6. Why showing only one option is a mistake
7. Why AI will make influence more important, not less
episodes.descriptionCN:
Jessica Fain 是 Webflow 的产品负责人，也是 Slack 的前首席产品官，她与 April Underwood 以及包括 Stewart Butterfield、Annie Pearl、Tamar Yehoshua 和 Noah Weiss 在内的许多过去的播客嘉宾一起工作。她的职业生涯一直在研究高管们实际上是如何做出决策的，以及为什么大多数人完全误解了这个过程。
我们讨论：
1. 为什么伟大的想法常常得不到认可
2. 为什么高管日历“就像闪光灯”以及为什么会议的前 30 秒如此重要
3. 为什么高管通常会针对全局最大化进行优化，而你则经常进行局部优化
4. 当领导者说出看似错误的事情时，杰西卡使用的最佳问题是：“这太有趣了。是什么让你相信这一点？”
5. 为什么你应该去学习，而不是去说服
6. 为什么只显示一个选项是错误的
7. 为什么人工智能会让影响力变得更加重要，而不是更少

episodes.title: The tactical playbook for getting 20-40% more comp (without sounding greedy) | Jacob Warwick (Executive Negotiator)
episodes.description:
Jacob Warwick is an executive negotiation coach who helps senior operators negotiate better salary, equity, titles, and severance packages. He has worked with leaders across tech and Hollywood, was previously a founder and CEO himself, and has helped clients secure millions in additional compensation. His approach focuses on collaboration over confrontation, understanding motivations, and treating job searches like enterprise sales processes.
We discuss:
1. Why a simple “What’s the chance there’s a little more here?” often unlocks a 20% bump
2. Why Jacob sees 40% average movement when negotiations are run well
3. When negotiation actually starts (hint: it’s much earlier than you think)
4. Why information + timing create power
5. The biggest mistakes people make when negotiating
6. How to navigate the important “What’s your comp expectation?” question without anchoring too low
7. Why the best interviews feel more like discovery calls than interrogations
episodes.descriptionCN:
雅各布·沃里克（Jacob Warwick）是一名高管谈判教练，帮助高级经营者谈判更好的薪资、股权、头衔和遣散费。他曾与科技界和好莱坞的领导者合作，此前他本人也是创始人兼首席执行官，并帮助客户获得了数百万美元的额外报酬。他的方法侧重于合作而非对抗、理解动机以及将求职视为企业销售流程。
我们讨论：
1. 为什么是一个简单的“这里还有什么机会？”通��会解锁 20% 的提升
2. 为什么雅各布认为谈判进展顺利时平均变动率为 40%
3. 谈判何时真正开始（提示：比你想象的要早得多）
4. 为什么信息+时机创造力量
5.人们在谈判时犯的最大错误
6. 如何应对重要的“你的薪酬期望是什么？”问题没有锚定太低
7. 为什么最好的采访感觉更像是发现电话而不是审讯

episodes.title: How I built a 1M+ subscriber newsletter and top 10 tech podcast | Lenny Rachitsky
episodes.description:
People have been asking me to sit on the other side of the mic for a long time. With my wife’s debut children’s book, Charts for Babies, coming out next month, we figured: why not do it together? What followed was one of the most honest conversations I’ve had on this podcast. Michelle asked things no one else would think to ask—and many things I’ve never shared publicly. You’ll hear about the specific moments that pushed me to start the newsletter, how I think about quality and iteration, what most stresses me out, and the scariest moment of my life. This was so fun, and so special, and I hope you like it.
We discuss:
1. The collection of moments that led me to what I do now
2. When I added a paywall, and how I knew it was working
3. The hidden treadmill behind shipping a newsletter post and podcast episode every week
4. The most stressful moments I’ve had in business and in life
5. How I think about stress, consistency, and keeping the business small
Pre-order Charts for Babies:
episodes.descriptionCN:
很长一段时间以来，人们一直要求我坐在麦克风的另一边。我妻子的首部儿童读物《婴儿图表》将于下个月出版，我们想：为什么不一起写呢？接下来是我在这个播客上进行过的最诚实的对话之一。米歇尔问了一些其他人不会想问的问题，还有很多我从未公开分享过的问题。您会听到促使我开始撰写时事通讯的具体时刻、我对质量和迭代的看法、给我带来最大压力的事情以及我一生中最可怕的时刻。这太有趣了，也太特别了，我希望你喜欢它。
我们讨论：
1. 引导我做出现在所做的事情的瞬间集合
2. 当我添加付费专区时，我如何知道它正在发挥作用
3. 每周发布时事通讯帖子和播客剧集背后隐藏的跑步机
4. 我在工作和生活中经历过的最有压力的时刻
5. 我如何看待压力、一致性和保持业务规模小
婴儿预购表：

episodes.title: Meet The Man Who Solved YouTube (With Data)
episodes.description:
Save 20% on 1of10 using code JAY20:
Schedule a 1of10 Strategy Call:
Video Notes and Checklists:
Richard is the co-founder of 1of10, a research platform built by YouTube strategists, and his team has quietly been behind the scenes for some of the biggest channels on the platform—helping creators accumulate over 2 billion views through a repeatable, data-backed system.
In this episode, Richard walks through his complete four-phase ideation system—audience identification, outlier research (using five distinct methods), idea remixing, and validation—and backs every step with real examples. We talk about what happens when the wrong audience floods your channel, why creators should double and triple down on formats that work, and how a single title change took one creator's video from 10,000 views to 150,000. He also shares data from 300,000+ YouTube outliers on the ideal title length (hint: shorter than you think) and where the sweet spots are for video duration across different niches.
🧠 CreatorHQ Operating System:
🧰 My Gear:
📬 Newsletter:
🧪 Join The Lab:
TIMESTAMPS
01:12 - Where 80-85% of YouTube success comes from
01:50 - Phase 1: Audience
03:19 - When should you start a fresh channel instead of pivoting?
04:09 - The danger of going viral with the wrong audience
05:40 - Phase 2: Research
07:37 - Format vs. Interest Topic
08:00 - Method 1: Inside your own channel
10:52 - Tripling and quadrupling down
12:33 - Method 2: Inside your niche
13:45 - Method 3: Adjacent niches
16:00 - Method 4: Outside your niche
17:37 - The "Japanese Rule" format
20:56 - Method 5: External inspiration
22:07 - Phase 3: Remixing

视频注释和清单：
Richard 是 1of10 的联合创始人，这是一个由 YouTube 战略家建立的研究平台，他的团队一直在该平台上一些最大频道的幕后默默工作，帮助创作者通过可重复的、有数据支持的系统积累超过 20 亿次观看次数。
在这一集中，理查德介绍了他完整的四阶段构思系统——受众识别、异常研究（使用五种不同的方法）、想法重新混合和验证——并用真实的例子支持每一步。我们讨论了当错误的观众涌入您的频道时会发生什么，为什么创作者应该在有效的格式上加倍或三倍的努力，以及一次标题更改如何使一个创作者的视频观看次数从 10,000 次增加到 150,000 次。他还分享了来自 300,000 多个 YouTube 异常值的数据，涉及理想的标题长度（提示：比您想象的短）以及不同细分市场的视频时长最佳点。

episodes.title: Don’t become a human AI wrapper.
Why Paul Millerd REFUSED a $200K Book Deal (And Self-Published Instead)
episodes.description:
Get Paul's New Hardcover:
Join The Lab:
Paul Millerd is the author of The Pathless Path, which has earned Paul $325,000 in royalties to date. In the process, Paul turned down a publishing offer from Penguin, one of the major publishers in the industry. Then, in December, Paul decided to double down and produce a new version of The Pathless Path, an ultra-premium hardcover book that is just beautiful.
In this episode, we talk about that decision, the dark side of traditional publishing, the book deal Paul WOULD have taken, and when traditional publishing actually makes sense.

episodes.descriptionCN:

加入实验室：
保罗·米勒德是《无路可走的道路》一书的作者，迄今为止，保罗已为保罗赚取了 325,000 美元的版税。在此过程中，保罗拒绝了业内主要出版商之一企鹅出版社的出版邀请。然后，在十二月，保罗决定加倍努力，制作新版本的《无路可走》，这是一本非常精美的超优质精装书。
在这一集中，我们将讨论这个决定、传统出版的阴暗面、保罗会采取的图书交易，以及传统出版何时真正有意义。

episodes.title: How To LEGALLY Pay Less Taxes (Money Expert Explains)
episodes.description:
Get Ankur's Free Tax Guide:
Sign up for Carry:
In 2020, Ankur Nagpal sold his course platform, Teachable, for around $250 million. But he knew that the sale would come with a giant tax bill. So Ankur became obsessed with the tax code, and he wanted to make it easier for everyone to legally operate within it. That led him to discover Solo 401(k)s, which he calls the most powerful tax account in America. So he built a new company called Carry that helps you legally keep more of what you earn and invest it the way you want.
This podcast is for informational purposes and not intended to be financial advice. Please consult a financial advisor about your specific situation.

episodes.title: I helped this YouTuber build a membership
episodes.description:
Join us in The Lab:
Meet Tommy. Tommy has a YouTube channel dedicated to helping Americans retire in France.
Tommy’s done an incredible job attracting nearly 30,000 subscribers and 2 million views on his channel, but he needed help dialing in his product offer.
Tommy is a member of The Lab, my invitation-only membership community for 6-and-7-figure creators. He scheduled a coaching call with me to work out how he can take his growing audience and design his product offers to both serve that audience and bring in serious revenue.
This is that coaching call, where we discuss how his membership, cohort-based courses, and retreats to France all work together.
